steps.title: Apply Keller's contrast
steps.content: Keller links point-of-view shots and invisible editing to viewer identification with a protagonist. She predicts the reverse effect when editing is conspicuous or point-of-view shots are absent: viewers become more critical of the protagonist.

steps.title: Reject unrelated viewer effects
steps.content: A contradicts conspicuous editing by saying artifice is obscured. C invents identification with the director. D mentions attention to style, but Keller's stated effect concerns the viewer's stance toward the protagonist. The answer is B.
